skill.name: agentic-browser
skill.description: Automate browser sessions through inference.sh with page navigation, element snapshots, screenshots, form interaction, and page JavaScript execution.
skill.summary: Control browser sessions with inference.sh for web automation, testing, scraping, and research workflows.
skill.author: inference-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many backtick and command-substitution patterns; most are Markdown code fences, inline code, or documented CLI examples rather than hidden execution. The remote installer pipe at SKILL.md line 20 is a confirmed high-risk supply-chain pattern, and the skill intentionally drives an external browser service over the network.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped Directly to Shell
high_findings.description: The quick start tells users to download a remote installer from cli.inference.sh and pipe it directly into sh before login. This is a true positive because the downloaded script would execute immediately without integrity verification or review.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is explicit on line 20. The domain appears related to the skill provider, but no checksum, signature, or pinned version is shown.

medium_findings.title: External Browser Automation With Network Interaction
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to run infsh commands that open URLs, submit forms, click elements, take screenshots, and keep browser sessions alive. This is legitimate browser automation, but it can transmit user-provided text and interact with third-party sites.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple documented examples show browser navigation and interaction through an external CLI. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the risk is contextual rather than evidence of malware.

medium_findings.title: Page JavaScript Execution Capability
medium_findings.description: The execute function runs JavaScript inside the active page context. This can be useful for extraction, but it also increases risk when used on authenticated pages or pages containing sensitive data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly documents a JavaScript execution function. The example is benign, but the capability is broad enough to require marketplace warning.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick and Weak-Crypto Static False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static findings point to Markdown code fences, inline code spans, table formatting, or ordinary description text. I did not find evidence that Ruby backtick execution or weak cryptographic algorithms are implemented in executable source code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown documentation and skill instructions, not Ruby or application source. The cited lines contain prose, shell examples, or table text rather than cryptographic code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The command downloads an installer over HTTPS and executes it directly with sh. Marketplace users should verify installer contents and integrity outside the skill before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This exact pattern appears in the quick start. It is dangerous even when the provider domain is expected because execution occurs before inspection.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Driven Web Actions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on repeated infsh CLI calls to control browser state, interact with pages, and run page scripts. These commands can cause network actions outside the local workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly use external CLI commands for browser activity. The actions are intentional features, but they are still important security-relevant patterns.

content.value_statement: Manual browser research and testing can slow down AI-assisted work. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured way to open pages, inspect elements, interact with forms, and capture screenshots.
actual_capabilities: Opens web pages through inference.sh browser sessions.
Returns page snapshots with interactive element references.
Clicks, fills, types, selects, hovers, scrolls, waits, and navigates back.
Captures page screenshots for visual review by agents.
Runs JavaScript in the active page for extraction tasks.
Maintains session state until the browser session is closed.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated infsh session.
Element references can expire after navigation or dynamic page updates.
Browser actions may affect real websites and accounts.
The skill does not provide built-in policy checks for scraping rules or site terms.

use_cases.title: Research Data Collection
use_cases.description: Open public pages, inspect visible elements, capture screenshots, and extract page text for later review.

use_cases.title: Form Workflow Testing
use_cases.description: Exercise login, contact, search, and submission flows using repeatable browser interactions.

use_cases.title: Agent Web Navigation
use_cases.description: Give an agent a session-based browser workflow that can navigate pages and refresh element snapshots.

prompt_templates.title: Open a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the agentic browser to open this URL, summarize the visible page state, and list the main interactive elements.

prompt_templates.title: Capture a Screenshot
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the target page, take a full-page screenshot, and describe the main layout and visible calls to action.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Form Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use a new browser session to fill the test form fields I provide, submit the form, then snapshot the result page.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Structured Page Signals
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the page, refresh the snapshot after load, run safe page JavaScript to collect heading and link text, then close the session.

output_examples.input: Open a product page and identify useful purchase controls.
output_examples.output: The page loaded with a product title, price area, quantity selector, add-to-cart button, and shipping information link.

output_examples.input: Take a screenshot after submitting a test search.
output_examples.output: The browser returned a results page screenshot and a refreshed element list containing filters, result links, and pagination controls.

output_examples.input: Extract headings from a documentation page.
output_examples.output: The result listed the main page title, section headings, and visible navigation labels for review.

best_practices: Use test accounts and non-sensitive data when filling forms.
Refresh the page snapshot after every navigation or dynamic update.
Close browser sessions after the task to limit persistent state.
anti_patterns: Do not run the remote installer without reviewing its source and integrity.
Do not enter passwords, payment data, or private tokens into untrusted pages.
Do not scrape sites without checking permission, rate limits, and legal requirements.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser navigation, element interaction, screenshots, page snapshots, and page JavaScript through inference.sh.

faq.question: Does it require an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. It depends on the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated infsh account.

faq.question: Can it fill forms?
faq.answer: Yes. It can fill, type, click, select, press keys, and submit flows when element references are available.

faq.question: Can element references be reused?
faq.answer: Only within the current page state. Refresh the snapshot after navigation, form submission, or dynamic content changes.

faq.question: Why is the security rating high?
faq.answer: The skill includes a remote installer piped to sh and intentionally performs external browser actions over the network.

faq.question: Is this safe for sensitive accounts?
faq.answer: Use caution. Prefer test accounts and avoid entering secrets or personal data unless the site and workflow are trusted.
